name: Dragon Reverse knowledge base
source_notes: Local corpus: Driver vulnerable, Driver suspect, Driver to test.
Public corpus model: LOLDrivers Living Off The Land Drivers. The plugin can optionally fetch https://www.loldrivers.io/api/drivers.json at runtime.
Rules produce triage hypotheses for authorized vulnerability research. They do not generate payloads or exploitation steps.

e4658d93544f69f5cb9aa6d9fec420fecc8750cb.name: AsmIo64.sys
e4658d93544f69f5cb9aa6d9fec420fecc8750cb.notes: Known BYOVD-style hardware access driver. Prioritize IOCTL access control, port IO, physical mapping, and MSR-like primitives.

4bf974f5d3489638a48ee508b4a8cfa0f0262909.name: AsrDrv.sys
4bf974f5d3489638a48ee508b4a8cfa0f0262909.notes: Known vulnerable hardware utility class. Prioritize weak device ACLs, IOCTL handlers, physical memory mapping, and registry writes.

b93020428f6d9ca700ab70a14911c7efcbcb1f9b.name: devMemDrv.sys
b93020428f6d9ca700ab70a14911c7efcbcb1f9b.notes: Physical memory access class. Prioritize MmMapIoSpace paths and user-controlled physical address selection.

70c9f9e8dcbba700e0fc20e6ae9b4c5df98326cf.name: Driver_win10.sys
70c9f9e8dcbba700e0fc20e6ae9b4c5df98326cf.notes: Known vulnerable sample in the local corpus. Use as a similarity seed for IOCTL and memory primitive patterns.

4429f32db1cc70567919d7d47b844a91cf1329a6.name: iqvw64e.sys
4429f32db1cc70567919d7d47b844a91cf1329a6.notes: Frequently abused BYOVD driver class. Prioritize arbitrary kernel memory access, physical mapping, and weak IOCTL access checks.

19a212e6fc324f4cb9ee5eba60f5c1fc0191799a.name: Phymemx64.sys
19a212e6fc324f4cb9ee5eba60f5c1fc0191799a.notes: Physical memory driver class. Prioritize mapping APIs and caller-controlled physical ranges.

03e0581432f5c8cc727a8aa387f5b69ff84d38d0.name: RTCore64.sys
03e0581432f5c8cc727a8aa387f5b69ff84d38d0.notes: Known BYOVD hardware driver class. Prioritize privileged read/write primitives, IOCTL access checks, and trust boundaries.

26f41e4268be59f5de07552b51fa52d18d88be94.name: VBoxDrv.sys
26f41e4268be59f5de07552b51fa52d18d88be94.notes: Local vulnerable corpus sample. Prioritize exposed device objects, IOCTL authorization, and kernel object reference patterns.

412564937dea61ad443a3f50a0f7586aed1bf687.name: whql.sys
412564937dea61ad443a3f50a0f7586aed1bf687.notes: Local vulnerable corpus sample. Use as a signed-driver similarity seed.

2628245d7886268f79b80907d364a6139312cfbc.name: ipt.sys
2628245d7886268f79b80907d364a6139312cfbc.notes: Local case: world-readable device plus privileged registry/service-key writes and Intel PT MSR programming. Prioritize weak device ACL, FILE_ANY_ACCESS wrappers, registry writes, and WRMSR/RDMSR paths.

families.name: Physical memory mapping primitive
families.description: Driver maps physical memory or locked pages and may accept ranges from IOCTL/session state.
families.review: Trace callers from IOCTL dispatchers to the mapping API. Verify address origin, range validation, ownership, cache type, and caller privilege checks.

families.name: MSR programming primitive
families.description: Driver reads or writes CPU model-specific registers from IOCTL-reachable code.
families.review: Locate WRMSR/RDMSR callers. Verify allowed MSR indexes, value masks, per-session ownership, and access requirements.

families.name: Raw port or MMIO access primitive
families.description: Driver exposes raw port IO or register IO to callers.
families.review: Check whether port/register offsets and widths are caller-controlled. Verify device ownership and privilege gates.

families.name: Privileged service-key registry write
families.description: Driver writes HKLM service configuration or other privileged registry state.
families.review: Check if a low-privileged device handle can cause privileged registry writes. Compare caller rights with driver-side writes.

families.name: Weak device object exposure
families.description: Driver creates a symbolic link or device object without clear secure ACL enforcement.
families.review: Recover the SDDL or default DACL. Verify whether Everyone/World can open the device and what access is required for each IOCTL.

families.name: METHOD_NEITHER or user-pointer trust
families.description: Driver may dereference caller pointers or copy buffers without consistent probing and bounds validation.
families.review: Inspect pointer provenance, try/except guards, buffer lengths, IOCTL method, and caller mode checks.

families.name: Kernel memory read/write primitive
families.description: Driver references processes, sections, MDLs, or memory copy APIs in IOCTL-reachable paths.
families.review: Trace whether source/destination address, process, section, or MDL parameters are caller-controlled and access-checked.

families.name: FILE_ANY_ACCESS IOCTL surface
families.description: One or more IOCTL constants decode to FILE_ANY_ACCESS, often widening reachability.
families.review: Decode IOCTLs. Privileged operations should require read/write access or explicit privilege checks, not FILE_ANY_ACCESS alone.

families.name: MDL, DMA, or locked-page exposure
families.description: Driver builds or maps MDLs, DMA buffers, scatter/gather lists, or contiguous memory that may cross trust boundaries.
families.review: Check whether the caller controls virtual addresses, lengths, process context, DMA direction, cache type, or mapping lifetime.

families.name: Process, token, or handle-sensitive operations
families.description: Driver references process, token, thread, or handle objects in ways that can become EoP when caller-controlled.
families.review: Trace PID/handle/token inputs from IOCTL buffers. Verify desired access, object type, AccessMode, PreviousMode, and privilege checks.

families.name: Process termination or protection bypass
families.description: Driver exposes or contains process termination, suspend, callback tamper, or protection-bypass behavior that may be abused against security tools.
families.review: Confirm caller-controlled PID/handle and missing authorization. For dynamic proof, use only a harmless owned process before making protected-process claims.

families.name: Security descriptor or DACL modification
families.description: Driver creates or modifies object ACLs, security descriptors, or grants access to device/service objects.
families.review: Recover the final ACL. Confirm whether World/Authenticated Users get read/write access to privileged device paths.

families.name: Security callback or filter tamper surface
families.description: Driver registers or interacts with process/thread/image callbacks, object callbacks, registry callbacks, or filter callbacks.
families.review: Check whether IOCTLs can disable, patch, unregister, or redirect callbacks and whether callback state is caller-controlled.

families.name: Firmware, PCI config, or bus access
families.description: Driver accesses PCI config space, firmware tables, SMBIOS, or low-level bus interfaces.
families.review: Verify whether bus/device/function, offset, width, and write values are caller-controlled. Check admin-only gating.

families.name: Unsafe copy or length arithmetic
families.description: Driver uses copy/format routines or arithmetic patterns that may create overflow, OOB, or info leak conditions.
families.review: Check integer truncation, signed/unsigned comparisons, output buffer lengths, and whether copy size derives from user input.

families.name: Information leak through debug or telemetry paths
families.description: Driver may disclose kernel pointers, physical addresses, MSRs, or privileged state through debug, ETW, WPP, or query IOCTLs.
families.review: Review query/readback paths for kernel pointer, physical address, handle, token, or privileged state disclosure to low-privileged callers.

families.name: Missing privilege gate around privileged primitive
families.description: A privileged primitive appears without nearby explicit privilege or IOCTL access validation signals.
families.review: Use this only with another primitive family. Confirm whether the branch rejects user-mode callers or only observes caller mode.

families.name: PhysicalMemory section or section-object mapping
families.description: Driver references section objects or physical-memory sections that may enable sensitive mapping or disclosure if IOCTL-reachable.
families.review: Rare/high-value axis: verify whether section names, handles, offsets, and protections are caller-controlled and whether user callers are rejected.

families.name: Firmware environment variable access
families.description: Driver can read or write UEFI/NVRAM-style firmware variables or system environment state.
families.review: Rare axis: validate variable name/GUID/value origin and whether SeSystemEnvironmentPrivilege or admin-only checks are enforced.

families.name: Legacy DMA adapter or scatter/gather control
families.description: Driver uses DMA adapter APIs where map registers, transfer lengths, or physical mappings may cross authorization boundaries.
families.review: Rare axis: check whether user input can steer DMA addresses, transfer length, direction, or common-buffer lifetime.

families.name: System-wide privileged configuration calls
families.description: Driver invokes privileged Zw/Nt system-information or debug-control operations that may become EoP when caller-controlled.
families.review: Rare/high-value axis: confirm class IDs, handles, buffers, and privilege checks. Build dynamic proof around direct-denial vs driver-mediated reachability.

families.name: Minifilter user communication boundary
families.description: Minifilter communication ports can expose privileged file-system actions or kernel data to user clients.
families.review: Check port SDDL, accepted clients, message schema, impersonation, and whether low-privileged clients can trigger privileged file-system actions.

families.name: IRP lifetime, cancel routine, or remove-lock race
families.description: Driver has cancellation, remove-lock, or work-item patterns that can create UAF/race candidates in complex IOCTL paths.
families.review: Rare bug-class axis: use this to guide code review and stress testing, not as a standalone vulnerability claim.

families.name: Hypervisor, VTL, or secure-kernel interface
families.description: Driver interacts with hypervisor/VTL/security-isolation interfaces where input validation bugs can have high impact.
families.review: Rare axis: map the interface boundary carefully. Look for user-controlled hypercall parameters, shared memory descriptors, or VMBus channel state.

families.name: Object namespace or symbolic-link confusion
families.description: Driver creates or opens named kernel objects, symbolic links, events, sections, or interfaces that can cross privilege boundaries.
families.review: Rare axis: inspect namespace, security descriptor, object lifetime, impersonation, and whether low-privileged users can pre-create or replace objects.

families.name: Impersonation or client-security boundary
families.description: Driver creates client security contexts or impersonates requestors, which can become EoP when requestor identity or access mode is mishandled.
families.review: Rare axis: verify impersonation level, requestor mode, token source, revert paths, and whether low-privileged clients can cause privileged filesystem/registry/object actions.

families.name: Executable mapping or kernel patch surface
families.description: Driver resolves kernel routines, changes page protections, or references executable mappings that may enable BYOVD-style patching when caller-controlled.
families.review: Rare/high-value axis: confirm whether addresses, routine names, protection flags, or MDLs are caller-controlled. Do not treat routine resolution alone as a vulnerability.

families.name: WMI, ETW, or telemetry control plane
families.description: Driver exposes WMI/ETW control or telemetry paths that may leak kernel state or trigger privileged behavior.
families.review: Rare axis: inspect provider security, query/set GUID handlers, event payloads, kernel pointer disclosure, and whether low-privileged clients can set privileged state.

families.name: ALPC or named port boundary
families.description: Driver or support component references ALPC named ports or message attributes that can cross user/kernel privilege boundaries.
families.review: Rare axis: verify port SDDL, accepted clients, message schema, impersonation, handle passing, and whether low-privileged clients can trigger privileged operations.
